turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: In our dissent. This is a constitutional tort action, a Bivens Action, as you heard. The plaintiffs claim that the conditions of their confinement after the September 11 attacks violated the Constitution and they seek monetary compensation. The majority concludes that the plaintiffs cannot, or in some instances may not be able to bring their suit even if they prove their allegations, and even if the defendants violated clearly established constitutional commands. Justice Ginsburg and I disagree. In our review, Bivens Actions should continue to provide appropriate compensation for those deprived of important constitutional rights and in times of special national security need. Bivens held that a plaintiff injured as a result of an unconstitutional search and seizure could bring a Damages Action against responsible federal officials. The Court's opinion, in our view rested in large part upon its traditional power to shape remedies for constitutional violations. A power that can be, and Bivens did just about trace that authority back to Marbury versus Madison itself. Subsequently, the Court authorized damages, as you have heard, for other constitutional violations. And although in recent years the Court has declined to expand the right to bring damage suits in, “fundamentally different” cases, the Court also made clear that it would not scale back Bivens' existing scope. Indeed where damages' remedy is questionable, the Court has provided a framework. Step one; ask whether a case arises in a new context. If so, that is the end of it. Bivens applies. But if not, stop it – no, if the context is new, go to step two, and ask whether there is a sufficient alternative existing process for protecting the relevant constitutional interest. And if there is not, go to step three, ask whether there are any special factors counseling hesitation. Here, Justice Ginsburg and I cannot get beyond step one. The plaintiffs' complaint focuses upon conditions of their confinement. It alleges that some or all of the plaintiffs were without justification shackled, confined in small unhygienic cells, subjected to continuous lighting presumably preventing sleep, frequently strip-searched, slammed against walls, and verbally abused. Some or all of the defendants, having turned a blind eye to this physical and mental abuse, or having themselves introduced or carried out policies that they knew would lead to these harms, all the while knowing that the plaintiffs had no connection to terrorism, but rather imposing these punitive conditions solely because of race and religion. In our opinion, we explain that there is nothing new about this kind of case. Nothing new or special about the defendants, the wardens and high-level department officials, about the plaintiffs, persons in confinement, or unfortunately, about the kinds of harms alleged. And remember, these are allegations. They have not been proved. Neither we explain, are there adequate alternative remedies available, nor are there other special factors counseling hesitation. That is done at some length in our opinion. But here and right now, we want to focus upon our main objection to what we believe is the most important point in the majority's opinion. And it is a point with which we strongly disagree. Namely, the majority's view that the post-9/11 circumstance, that national security emergency does or might well constitute a special factor precluding a damages remedy. We understand the majority's basic concern namely, that federal officials might hesitate in carrying out their security-related responsibilities if they are afraid of lawsuits for damages with an attendant risk of judicial second-guessing of decisions that executive officials thought were reasonable and necessary at the time. We nonetheless do not believe that this consideration warrants doing away with constitutional tort actions in the 9/11 national security context. Well, why not? For one thing, the Bivens Action comes equipped with a host of legal doctrines, other legal doctrines that offer federal officials a protective shield. To win their case, plaintiffs must show, they cannot simply allege, they have to show that the Constitution was in fact violated. And as Justice Jackson pointed out, the Constitution is not a suicide pact, its provisions are flexible. The Fourth Amendment for example, forbids unreasonable searches and seizures. Its warrant requirement often gives way to emergency needs, and what may count as unreasonable in time of peace, may prove necessary in time of war or national security emergency. Moreover, a Bivens Action comes accompanied with a qualified immunity defense. The defendants are subject to suit only if the rights that are alleged, they supposedly, they allegedly violated, are “clearly established.” And again, prohibitions that are clearly established in time of peace may not be clearly established in time of war or security emergency. Further, a complaint has to state a claim that is plausible. Conclusory Statements, threadbare allegations, they will not do. And finally, courts can and should structure discovery orders in ways that minimalize the intrusive impact on the work of federal officials, always keeping in mind that qualified immunity is immunity from suit as well as immunity from liability. These safeguards recognize that the Constitution delegates the power to protect the nation to the executive and legislative branches of government, not to the judiciary. But given these safeguards, why abolish the constitutional tort action itself? If you are cold, put on a sweater, maybe an overcoat, perhaps even turn up the heat. But do not set fire to the house. Moreover, in time of war or national security emergency, Bivens Actions may be especially particularly needed. History warns us of the risk to liberty in times of national crises. We have read about the Alien and Sedition Acts, the suppression of civil liberties during World War I, the unnecessary internment during World War II of 70,000 American citizens of Japanese origin. The majority says that suits for injunctive relief are adequate protection against the risk of government overreach. But courts may prove particularly hesitant to interfere with the ongoing actions of other branches during the war or emergency itself, especially if they then lack relevant information. A damages action however is typically brought after the emergency is over, after emotions have cooled, and at a time when more factual information is available. At that point, courts have time to exercise the judicial virtues of calm reflection and dispassionate application of the law to the facts. In the cases brought by the Guantanamo Detainees and in the Steel Seizure Case brought during the Korean War, this Court applied the Constitution to actions taken during national security emergency. The wisdom of permitting courts to hear suits after the emergency has passed follows a fortiori. For then, the courts at least can see that there is compensation, a degree of vindication for those whose constitutional rights were, at the time clearly violated. As is well known, Lord Atkins, a British judge, wrote in the midst of World War II that, “Amid the clash of arms, the laws are not silent. They may be changed, but they speak the same language in war as in peace.” The Court should say the same here. In our view, it should sustain these Bivens Actions. For these reasons and others set out in our opinion, Justice Ginsburg and I dissent.
